evidence_text: Only 466 repositories, or 5% of the scanned repositories, had adopted at least one of the four AI configuration file formats considered.
source_excerpt: "Only 466 (5%) of the repositories that we scanned had already adopted at least one of the formats that we considered."
note: The reported adoption rate is limited to the four file formats included in the study.
caveat: The reported adoption rate is limited to the four file formats included in the study.

evidence_text: The preliminary study selected 10,000 repositories from a final filtered sample of 49,311 repositories.
source_excerpt: "This resulted in a final sample of 49,311 repositories. Our long-term goal is to collect AI configuration files from all these repositories. For this work-in-progress paper, we selected 10,000 repositories based on a custom ranking approach that balances the popularity and maturity of the projects."
note: The paper describes the 10,000-repository set as a preliminary selection from a larger filtered pool.
caveat: The paper describes the 10,000-repository set as a preliminary selection from a larger filtered pool.

evidence_text: The repository scan looked for four AI configuration file types supported by the GitHub Copilot coding agent: Copilot instructions, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and GEMINI.md.
source_excerpt: "We cloned all repositories and scanned their default branch to find all types of configuration files that the GitHub Copilot coding agent supports (GitHub, 2025b): Copilot instructions, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and GEMINI.md."
note: This evidence describes the file types included in the scan, not all possible AI agent configuration formats.
caveat: This evidence describes the file types included in the scan, not all possible AI agent configuration formats.

evidence_text: Among repositories with AI configuration files, the counts by main language were 135 TypeScript, 58 Go, 58 Python, 56 C#, 36 Java, 34 JavaScript, 32 C++, 29 Rust, 19 PHP, and 9 C.
citation: Mohsenimofidi et al., 2026, RQ1 language distribution
source_excerpt: "We found AI configuration files in 135 repositories with TypeScript as main language, 58 Go, 58 Python, 56 C#, 36 Java, 34 JavaScript, 32 C++, 29 Rust, 19 PHP, and 9 C."
note: The counts refer to repositories with AI configuration files in the authors' sample.
caveat: The counts refer to repositories with AI configuration files in the authors' sample.

evidence_text: Copilot instruction files had the highest average length at 310 lines (SD 127), while AGENTS.md files averaged 142 lines with the highest variation (SD 231).
citation: Mohsenimofidi et al., 2026, RQ1 file lengths
source_excerpt: "The Copilot instruction files were on average the longest ( M = 310 M=310 lines, S D = 127 SD=127 lines), followed by CLAUDE.md files ( M = 287 M=287 , S D = 112 SD=112 ). AGENTS.md files had the highest variation ( M = 142 M=142 , S D = 231 SD=231 )."
note: This evidence compares average file lengths and variation across file types in the sampled repositories.
caveat: This evidence compares average file lengths and variation across file types in the sampled repositories.

evidence_text: AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md were the most common co-occurring pair of AI configuration files, appearing together in 25 repositories.
source_excerpt: "We also looked at which files most commonly co-occured, finding that AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md was the most common pair (in 25 repositories)."
note: The paper reports pairwise co-occurrence, not broader multi-file combinations.
caveat: The paper reports pairwise co-occurrence, not broader multi-file combinations.

evidence_text: The RQ2 heading analysis started from 155 AGENTS.md files, excluded 15 files created before January 1, 2025, and excluded 5 files without heading structure.
citation: Mohsenimofidi et al., 2026, RQ2 heading analysis
source_excerpt: "To answer RQ2, we extracted all section headings from the 155 AGENTS.md file in our sample... We excluded 15 AGENTS.md files that were created before January 1, 2025, i.e., before the AGENTS.md convention was introduced... Five repositories contained AGENTS.md files without any heading structure; these were excluded from the analysis."
note: These exclusions applied to the heading-structure analysis, not necessarily to all other analyses in the paper.
caveat: These exclusions applied to the heading-structure analysis, not necessarily to all other analyses in the paper.

evidence_text: The initial heading analysis was restricted to level 1 and level 2 headings because prior README work found those levels most informative and consistent.
citation: Mohsenimofidi et al., 2026, RQ2 scope restriction
source_excerpt: "We recorded the heading levels (from #, i.e., level 1, to #####, i.e., level 5) to understand the structural depth of the documents. Following related work on README files (Gao et al., 2025), which found that the first- and second-level headings are the most informative and consistent, we restricted our initial analysis to the headings on levels 1 and 2."
note: This evidence describes an analysis choice that bounded the scope of the heading study.
caveat: This evidence describes an analysis choice that bounded the scope of the heading study.

evidence_text: The three most frequent level 1 or 2 AGENTS.md heading categories were Conventions/best practices (50), Contribution guidelines (48), and Architecture and project structure (47).
source_excerpt: "Table 1. Categories of information provided in AGENTS.md files... Conventions/best practices ... 50 ... Contribution guidelines ... 48 ... Architecture and project structure ... 47"
note: The counts are the number of level 1 or 2 headings in the coded category table.
caveat: The counts are the number of level 1 or 2 headings in the coded category table.

evidence_text: The authors characterized AGENTS.md writing style along five dimensions: descriptive, prescriptive, prohibitive, explanatory, and conditional.
citation: Mohsenimofidi et al., 2026, RQ2 writing styles
source_excerpt: "We found that the writing style can be characterized along five stylistic dimensions: descriptive, prescriptive, prohibitive, explanatory, and conditional."
note: This evidence describes an observed style taxonomy, not a tested causal account of how style changes agent behavior.
caveat: This evidence describes an observed style taxonomy, not a tested causal account of how style changes agent behavior.

evidence_text: The paper reports that AGENTS.md files vary widely in both information content and presentation, without a consistent structure.
source_excerpt: "In summary, AGENTS.md files vary widely in both the information they contain and how it is presented... Projects often document architecture, contribution processes, and coding conventions—but without a consistent structure."
note: This evidence carries a structural limitation: recurring topics exist, but the paper explicitly reports no consistent organizational structure.
caveat: This evidence carries a structural limitation: recurring topics exist, but the paper explicitly reports no consistent organizational structure.

evidence_text: Among 155 AGENTS.md files, 77 files (50%) were never changed after the initial commit, 36 (23%) changed once, and 32 (21%) changed between two and seven times.
citation: Mohsenimofidi et al., 2026, RQ3 change frequency
source_excerpt: "To answer RQ3, we first screened the commit histories of all 155 AGENTS.md files and found that 77 (50%) of them had not been changed after the initial commit, 36 (23%) only once, and 32 (21%) between two and seven times."
note: These percentages describe post-initial-commit change frequency in the AGENTS.md files examined for RQ3.
caveat: These percentages describe post-initial-commit change frequency in the AGENTS.md files examined for RQ3.

evidence_text: For detailed RQ3 analysis, the authors focused on 10 files with at least 10 commits, producing a sample of 169 commits, which was 37% of 453 collected commits.
citation: Mohsenimofidi et al., 2026, RQ3 high-change subset
source_excerpt: "For this study, we decided to focus on 10 files (6%) with at least 10 commits, which yielded a sample of 169 commits to annotate (37% of the 453 collected commits)."
note: This evidence describes the subsample used for detailed commit annotation.
caveat: This evidence describes the subsample used for detailed commit annotation.

evidence_text: In the high-change AGENTS.md subset, the most frequent change categories were Add instruction(s) with 78 occurrences and Modify instruction(s) with 59 occurrences.
source_excerpt: "Only 466 (5%) of the repositories that we scanned had already adopted at least one of the formats that we considered."
note: The reported adoption rate is limited to the four file formats included in the study.
caveat: The reported adoption rate is limited to the four file formats included in the study.
